name: sh40-slate
documentation:
![slate](https://singlecolorimage.com/get/717E8D/1600x200) ## Slate A powerful terminal management library. ``` pip install sh40-slate ``` ![rule](https://singlecolorimage.com/get/717E8D/1600x3) ### Purpose `Slate` handles most of the interactions one might be expected to do while writing a UI application for terminals (TUI). It does so in a simple to understand, Pythonic manner, and uses a simple synchronous API to allow for maximum compatibility with existing codebases. ![rule](https://singlecolorimage.com/get/717E8D/1600x3) ### Feature highlights #### Screen API Under the hood, `Slate`'s terminal objects each have a `Screen`. This screen is used for creating fast diffs of the display state between renders, allowing you to draw _only_ the parts that changed. Anyone who's worked with terminals before knows their rendering is almost always the primary bottleneck of any application. They are surprisingly awful at rendering a full-screen's result, _especially_ when clearing the display beforehand. Many applications result to hand-crafted update systems that only redraw the widgets that change, but that can still be quite intensive based on the size and content of each. `Slate` handles cell-level diffing without you ever having to think about it. You just draw content to the terminal, and we will keep track of all the changes to display on the next draw, with practically 0 performance hit. ```python import time from random import randint from slate import terminal, Span, Color # Hide the terminal's cursor terminal.show_cursor(False) content = [ Span( "X" * terminal.width, bold=True, foreground=Color.black().lighten(2), background=Color.black(is_background=True).lighten(1), ) for _ in range(terminal.height) ] # Use an alt buffer to keep the pre-run terminal state intact with terminal.alt_buffer(): for line in content: terminal.write(content) terminal.draw() # Draw a '0' to a random spot on the screen, 60 times a second # # This type of operation is really taxing on terminals (especially old ones, # like Terminal.app) as it generally is done by redrawing the entire screen. # That's no longer gonna be a problem for us. while True: cursor = (randint(0, terminal.width - 1), randint(0, terminal.height - 1)) # Make sure no more than 1 cell changes; a sanity check for the most part, # as this setup guarantees it. assert terminal.write("0", cursor=cursor) <= 1 terminal.draw() time.sleep(1 / 60) ``` #### Terminal's smart cursor If you paid attention, you might have noticed that we never moved the terminal's cursor while drawing the initial lines of content. This is because the terminal will always track and move its own cursor when written to, so it knows when it ran out of space and needs to move to a new line. ```python from slate import terminal print(terminal.cursor) # (0, 0) terminal.write("1") print(terminal.cursor) # (1, 0) ``` It will even wrap while writing! ```python from slate import terminal, getch # It will even wrap while writing! terminal.write("#" * (terminal.width - 5)) terminal.write("This is long, but it will wrap around to the next line.") terminal.draw() getch() # Wait for input so the shell prompt doesn't slide things out of view ``` #### Sophisticated color tools You might have noticed the usage of the lighten and darken API for colors above. These are amongst the many tools we provide for working with colors, which also include blending, generating W3C guidelines compliant contrast colors (white for dark colors, black for light ones), generating entire 4-color palettes with multiple of the most commonly used strategies, and more. Best part; **it works everywhere**. Colors get translated to the best approximation the current terminal can display, so you don't have to worry about things looking completely wonky without true color support. Most terminals (at least ones you should be using / targeting) have true color support nowadays, but our approximations will be _good enough_ to use on older ones as well. We also have advanced support for the [NO_COLOR](https://no-color.org/) initiative, but instead of completely stripping the semantic information colors can convey, we translate each color to a greyscale value that matches with the _perceived_ [lightness](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lightness), keeping the application informative.

### Documentation Once the library gets to a settled state (near 1.0), documentation will be hosted both online and as a celx application. Until then peep the `examples` folder, or check out some of the references by using `python3 -m pydoc `. ### See also This library is mostly supposed to _power_ some higher level tools, so using it raw might not be ideal. Thankfully, we have two projects that can help with that: - [Zenith](https://github.com/shade40/zenith): A markup language with palette generation, built on `Slate`'s `Span` and `Color` primitives. - [Celadon](https://github.com/shade40/celadon): A TUI library that uses `Slate`'s `Terminal` to handle _all_ terminal-interfacing. - [celx](https://github.com/shade40/celx): A hypermedia-driven TUI framework built on top of `Celadon`.

modules.name: slate
documentation: A lightweight set of tools for interfacing with the terminal.

modules.name: slate.terminal
documentation: The Terminal class, which is the primary surface to interact with the emulator.

classes.name: slate.terminal.Terminal

methods.name: slate.terminal.Terminal.color_space
documentation:
Returns the best color space available on the system.

If `NO_COLOR` is set, ColorSpace.NO_COLOR is returned.

methods.name: slate.terminal.Terminal.color_space
documentation: Overrides the queried color space setting.

methods.name: slate.terminal.Terminal.supports_synchronized_update
documentation:
Queries whether this terminal supports synchronized output.

https://gist.github.com/christianparpart/d8a62cc1ab659194337d73e399004036

methods.name: slate.terminal.Terminal.foreground_color
documentation: Returns the terminal's default foreground color.

methods.name: slate.terminal.Terminal.background_color
documentation: Returns the terminal's default background color.

methods.name: slate.terminal.Terminal.size
documentation: Returns the size (width, height) of the terminal.

methods.name: slate.terminal.Terminal.width
documentation: Returns the width of the terminal.

methods.name: slate.terminal.Terminal.height
documentation: Returns the height of the terminal.

methods.name: slate.terminal.Terminal.isatty
documentation: Returns whether this terminal represents a TTY.

methods.name: slate.terminal.Terminal.resolution
documentation:
Gets the pixel resolution of the terminal.

Args:
    fallback: Returned when something went wrong. Usually this means the
        terminal is not a TTY, or it sent a garbled response.

Returns:
    A tuple of (width, height) of the terminal 'screen', in pixels.

methods.name: slate.terminal.Terminal.cursor
documentation: Sets/gets the terminal's screen's cursor.

methods.name: slate.terminal.Terminal.cursor
documentation: Sets/gets the terminal's screen's cursor.

methods.name: slate.terminal.Terminal.alt_buffer
documentation: Manages an alternate buffer in the terminal.

methods.name: slate.terminal.Terminal.no_echo
documentation: Temporarily disables echoing in the terminal.

methods.name: slate.terminal.Terminal.report_mouse
documentation: Sets mouse reporting for the duration of the context manager.

methods.name: slate.terminal.Terminal.batch
documentation:
A context within which every `write` is batched into one draw call.

This implements the [synchronized update]
(https://gist.github.com/christianparpart/d8a62cc1ab659194337d73e399004036)
protocol.

methods.name: slate.terminal.Terminal.set_title
documentation:
Sets the terminal's title.

The first time it is set within an application, the previous value will be
stored. When the application finishes (i.e. `alt_buffer` quits) the original
title is restored.

methods.name: slate.terminal.Terminal.show_cursor
documentation: Shows or hides the terminal's cursor.

methods.name: slate.terminal.Terminal.set_report_mouse
documentation: Starts or stops listening to SGR 1006 mouse events.

methods.name: slate.terminal.Terminal.clear
documentation:
Clears the screen's entire matrix.

Args:
    fillchar: The character to fill the matrix with.

methods.name: slate.terminal.Terminal.write
documentation:
Writes a span to the screen at the given cursor position.

Args:
    data: The data to write. If passed as a string, `Span.yield_from` is used
        to get a generator of `Span` objects.
    cursor: The location of the screen to start writing at, anchored to the
        top-left. If not given, the screen's last used cursor is used.
    force_overwrite: If set, each of the characters written will be registered
        as a change.

Returns:
    The number of cells that have been updated as a result of the write.

methods.name: slate.terminal.Terminal.write_control
documentation:
Writes some control sequence to the terminal.

This method writes directly to the stream, bypassing the screen mechanism.

Args:
    sequence: The control sequence to write.
    flush: If set, the stream will be flushed after writing.

methods.name: slate.terminal.Terminal.draw
documentation:
Draws the current screen to the terminal.

Args:
    redraw: If set, the screen will do a complete redraw, instead of only
        writing changes.

methods.name: slate.terminal.Terminal.bell
documentation: Plays the terminal's bell sound.

methods.name: slate.terminal.Terminal.export_svg
documentation:
Exports an SVG image that represents this terminal.

Internally, this exports the terminal's screen and wraps it in a terminal-like
style.

Args:
    font_size: The font size used for the screen's content.
    default_foreground: For each character on the screen, this color is used
        when it doesn't have a foreground color.
    default_background: For each character on the screen, this color is used
        when it doesn't have a background color.
An object to read & write data to and from the terminal.

modules.name: slate.core
documentation:
A set of non-object specific terminal API implementations.

Most of these are best used from the `Terminal` object.

functions.name: slate.core._build_event
documentation:
Generates a mouse event's encoded identifier.

Args:
    name: The event name to add as a sufix.
    base: The index where the event starts.
    has_alternate: If set, a "left_" and "right_" pair of events will be
        generated, instead of just one with no prefix.

Returns:
    A dictionary of the event's encoded identifier (as str) mapping to the
    event's name. Event names are formatted as:

        {modifier}_{event}

    or in the case of `has_alternate`:

        {modifier}_left_{event}
        {modifier}_right_{event}

    ...where `modifier` is assembled least to most powerful, in the order:

        nothing < shift < ctrl < option

    meaning `option` will always be the last modifier, and `shift` the first.

functions.name: slate.core.width
documentation:
Returns the visual width of some text.

Args:
    text: The text to test for. Spans are faster to query, as they don't need to
        rely on regex for the calculation.

functions.name: slate.core.get_default_color
documentation:
Gets the default fore or back color for the terminal attached to the stream.

Args:
    layer: The color layer. 10 for foreground color, 11 for background.
    stream: The stream to write the query to. Uses `sys.stdout` by default.

Return:
    A hexadecimal triplet with the format `rrggbb`. Note that it doesn't include
    a leading hash.

functions.name: slate.core.get_color_space
documentation:
Gets the maximum supported color system supported by the shell environment.

Returns:
    The highest-supported color system in the terminal. If `$NO_COLOR` is set,
    `ColorSpace.NO_COLOR` will always be returned.

functions.name: slate.core.parse_mouse_event
documentation:
Parses a mouse event.

Args:
    code: The event sequence sent by the terminal.

Returns:
    None if the code cannot be parsed as a mouse event, otherwise
    a string with the format:

        mouse:{event_name}@{event_x};{event_y}

functions.name: slate.core._is_ready
documentation:
Determines if IO object is reading to read.

Args:
    file: An IO object of any type.

Returns:
    A boolean describing whether the object has unread
    content.

functions.name: slate.core.timeout
documentation:
Allows context to run for a certain amount of time, quits it once it's up.

Note that this should never be run on Windows, as the required signals are not
present. Whenever this function is run, there should be a preliminary OS check,
to avoid running into issues on unsupported machines.

functions.name: slate.core.feed
documentation:
Feeds some text to be read by `getch`.

This can be used to manually "interrupt" an ongoing getch call.

functions.name: slate.core._getch_posix
documentation:
Implementation for getting characters on POSIX systems.

Args:
    stream: The stream to read from. Defaults to `sys.stdin`.

Returns:
    The maximum-length sequence of characters that could be read.

functions.name: slate.core._getch_nt
documentation:
Implementation for getting characters on NT systems.

Returns:
    The maximum-length sequence of characters that could be read.

functions.name: slate.core.getch
documentation:
Gets characters from the stream, without blocking.

The implementation is different on POSIX and NT systems, and this method
always selects the correct one.

Args:
    stream: The stream to read from. Defaults to `sys.stdin`. Note that this is
        not used on Windows, as the `msvcrt.getch` function does not support
        alternate streams.
    raw: If set, the returned string will not be converted to its canonical name,
        e.g. '\x1b[D' -> 'left' or '\x01' -> 'ctrl-a'.

Returns:
    The longest sequence of characters that could be read from the stream.

functions.name: slate.core.getch_timeout
documentation:
Calls `getch` with a system timeout.

Note that this on Windows this will call `getch` normally, as `SIGALRM` is not
supported on those systems.

Args:
    duration: The timeout's length, in seconds.
    default: What to return if no input is given within the duration.
    stream: The stream to read from. Defaults to `sys.stdin`. Note that this is
        not used on Windows, as the `msvcrt.getch` function does not support
        alternate streams.
    raw: If set, the returned string will not be converted to its canonical name,
        e.g. '\x1b[D' -> 'left' or '\x01' -> 'ctrl-a'.

Returns:
    The result of `getch` if input was given before the timeout,
    otherwise `default`.

functions.name: slate.core.set_echo
documentation:
Sets the terminal's ECHO flag to the given value.

Currently not supported on windows.

classes.name: slate.core.Key
documentation:
The object returned by `getch`.

This allows for checking equality against multiple possible keyboard inputs,
like `ctrl-i` and `tab`, through the same object.

classes.name: slate.core.ColorSpace

class_variables.name: slate.core.ColorSpace.NO_COLOR
documentation: Set by the `$NO_COLOR` shell variable; convert all colors to greyscale.

class_variables.name: slate.core.ColorSpace.STANDARD
documentation: Only the 16 standard colors are supported.

class_variables.name: slate.core.ColorSpace.EIGHT_BIT
documentation: 256 colors are supported.

class_variables.name: slate.core.ColorSpace.TRUE_COLOR
documentation: Full RGB color support is available.
The color space supported by the terminal.

modules.name: slate.key_names
documentation: Dictionaries of key translations, used by `getch` and friends.

modules.name: slate.__about__
documentation: Meta information about the library.

modules.name: slate.color
documentation: The Color class, and some related utilities.

functions.name: slate.color.geometric_difference
documentation:
Gets the geometric difference of 2 RGB triplets.

See https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Color_difference's Euclidian section.

functions.name: slate.color.calculate_luminance
documentation:
Calculates the given color's luminance (objective brightness).

Source: https://stackoverflow.com/a/596243.

functions.name: slate.color.calculate_brightness
documentation:
Returns the given color's brightness (perceived luminance)..

From https://stackoverflow.com/a/56678483

functions.name: slate.color.calculate_contrast
documentation:
Calculates the given color's black/white contrast.

Args:
    base: The color that's used for the calculation.
    white: The color returned for "dark" bases. Defaults to (245, 245, 245).
    black: The color returned for "light" bases. Defaults to (35, 35, 35).

Returns:
    A color that satisfies W3C's contrast guidelines when painted on top of `base`.

functions.name: slate.color.calculate_triadic_group
documentation: Returns the triadic group this color is within.

functions.name: slate.color.calculate_analogous_group
documentation: Returns the analogous group this color is within.

functions.name: slate.color.calculate_tetradic_group
documentation: Returns the triadic group this color is within.

functions.name: slate.color.color

type.name: str | tuple[int, int, int] | tuple[int, int, int, float]

documentation:
Creates a color from the given description.

This calls either the Color constructor, `Color.from_ansi` or
`Color.from_hex`, depending on the given value.

classes.name: slate.color.Color

methods.name: slate.color.Color.from_ansi
documentation: Creates a color instance from an ANSI sequence's body.

methods.name: slate.color.Color.from_hex
documentation: Generates a Color instance from a hex string.

methods.name: slate.color.Color.black
documentation: Returns a 100% black.

methods.name: slate.color.Color.white
documentation: Returns a 100% white.

methods.name: slate.color.Color.ansi
documentation: Returns the ANSI code that represents this color.

methods.name: slate.color.Color.contrast
documentation: Returns this given color's black/white contrast color.

methods.name: slate.color.Color.complement
documentation: Returns the complement of this color.

methods.name: slate.color.Color.triadic_group
documentation: Returns the triadic group this color is part of.

methods.name: slate.color.Color.analogous_group
documentation: Returns the analogous group this color is part of.

methods.name: slate.color.Color.tetradic_group
documentation: Returns the tetradic group this color is part of.

methods.name: slate.color.Color.as_alpha
documentation: Returns this color with the given alpha.

methods.name: slate.color.Color.lighten
documentation:
Returns a lighter version of this color.

The calculation is done by blending white with an alpha of:

    `shade_count * step_size`

methods.name: slate.color.Color.darken
documentation:
Returns a darker version of this color.

The calculation is done by blending black with an alpha of:

    `shade_count * step_size`

methods.name: slate.color.Color.as_background
documentation: Returns this color, with the given background setting.

methods.name: slate.color.Color.hue_shift
documentation:
Returns a color with a hue offset of the given amount.

Args:
    amount: The difference in hue. Given as 0-1f.

Returns:
    This color, with its hue shifted.

methods.name: slate.color.Color.blend
documentation:
Blends this color with other by a certain alpha.

Args:
    other: The color to blend into.
    alpha: How closely we should blend to the given color. For example,
        0.9 would be 10% self, 90% other.

Returns:
    The blended color.

methods.name: slate.color.Color.blend_complement
documentation:
Blends this color by its complement.

See `blend` for more info.

Args:
    alpha: How closely we should blend into the complement.
A class that represents an RGB value.

modules.name: slate.event
documentation: The basic Event class used by the rest of the library.

classes.name: slate.event.CallbackError
documentation: Raised when something went wrong with an event's callback

classes.name: slate.event.Event

methods.name: slate.event.Event.__bool__
documentation: Returns whether this event has any listeners.

methods.name: slate.event.Event.__call__
documentation:
Emits the event to all listeners.

Args:
    data: The content of the event.

Returns:
    Whether any callbacks returned True.

methods.name: slate.event.Event.append
documentation: Adds a new listener.

methods.name: slate.event.Event.clear
documentation: Removes all listeners from th event.

An emittable event.

Construct an event and store it in a variable to have a reference for it. Then, you
can `+=` callback handlers onto it, and call the event object with data to notify
all of the callbacks.

modules.name: slate.screen
documentation: The Screen class, which allows for smart overwrite-based terminal drawing.

functions.name: slate.screen._get_blended
documentation: Returns `base` blended by `other` at `other`'s alpha value.

classes.name: slate.screen.ChangeBuffer

methods.name: slate.screen.ChangeBuffer.clear
documentation: Clears the buffer.

methods.name: slate.screen.ChangeBuffer.gather
documentation:
Gathers all changes.

Returns:
    A list of items in the format:

        (x, y), changed_str
A simple class that keeps track of x, y positions of changed characters.

classes.name: slate.screen.Screen

methods.name: slate.screen.Screen.resize
documentation:
Resizes the cell matrix to a new size.

Args:
    size: The new size.

methods.name: slate.screen.Screen.clear
documentation:
Clears the screen's entire matrix.

Args:
    fillchar: The character to fill the matrix with.

methods.name: slate.screen.Screen.write
documentation:
Writes data to the screen at the given cursor position.

Args:
    spans: Any iterator of Span objects.
    cursor: The location of the screen to start writing at, anchored to the
        top-left. If not given, the screen's last used cursor is used.
    force_overwrite: If set, each of the characters written will be registered
        as a change.

Returns:
    The number of cells that have been updated as a result of the write.

methods.name: slate.screen.Screen.render
documentation:
Collects all buffered changes and returns them as a single string.

Args:
    origin: The offset to apply to all positions.

methods.name: slate.screen.Screen.export_svg_with_styles
documentation:
Exports a whole load of SVG tags that represents our character matrix.

Args:
    font_size: The font size within the SVG.
    origin: The origin of all the coordinates in the output.
    default_foreground: If a character doesn't have a foreground, this gets
        substituted.
    default_background: If a character doesn't have a foreground, this gets
        substituted.
    style_class_template: The template used to create classes for each unique
        style. Must contain `{i}`.

Returns:
    The output SVG, and all the styles contained within. Note that the SVG
    here is only the body, so you need to wrap it as `<svg ...>{here}</svg>`.
    Terminal does this automatically.

A matrix of cells that represents a 'screen'.

This matrix keeps track of changes between each draw, so only the newest changes
are written to the terminal. This helps eliminate full-screen redraws.

modules.name: slate.color_info
documentation:
Tables of lots of colors.

`COLOR_TABLE` contains all 256 8-bit colors' RGB values, as defined by standards.

`CSS_COLORS` contains all modern CSS colors' RGB values.

modules.name: slate.span
documentation: The Span class, a piece of formatted text for the terminal.

functions.name: slate.span._escape_html
documentation: Escapes HTML and replaces ' ' with  .

functions.name: slate.span._is_block
documentation:
Determines whether the given text only contains block characters.

These characters reside in the unicode range of 9600-9631, which is what we test
against.

functions.name: slate.span._is_background_color
documentation:
Determines whether the given color body is a background.

This assumes that the given body is already color.

functions.name: slate.span._is_valid_color
documentation:
Determines whether the given sequence body is a valid color.

It recognizes the following:

- Standard colors in ranges (30, 38), (40, 48), (90, 97), (100, 107), not
    including the endpoint
- RGB colors (`38;2;xxx` or `48;2;xxx`)
- 8-bit colors (`38;5;xxx` or `48;5;xxx`)

functions.name: slate.span._parse_sequence
documentation:
Parses the given sequence and inserts it into the given SpanConfig..

This modifies the given options dictionary.

Returns:
    Either the first un-parseable part, or None if everything went well.

classes.name: slate.span.SpanConfig
documentation: A dictionary used to store span configuration.

classes.name: slate.span.Span

methods.name: slate.span.Span._generate_sequences
documentation: Generates the sequences represented by this Span.

methods.name: slate.span.Span.attrs
documentation: Returns a copy of the attributes that define this span.

methods.name: slate.span.Span.yield_from
documentation: Generates Span objects from the given line.

methods.name: slate.span.Span.first_from
documentation: Returns the first object generated by `yield_from_line`.

methods.name: slate.span.Span.group
documentation:
Groups similar characters next to eachother into spans.

Two characters are similar if their styles match.

methods.name: slate.span.Span.get_characters
documentation:
Splits the Span into its characters.

Args:
    always_include_sequence: Include the span's sequences before every
        character. If not set, only the first character will have them.

Returns:
    A list of strings with the format `{sequences}{plain_character}`.

methods.name: slate.span.Span.get_svg_styles
documentation: Returns this span's styling converted to SVG-compatible CSS.

methods.name: slate.span.Span.as_svg
documentation:
Exports this span into a set of SVG tags (`<rect>`, `<text>`).

Args:
    font_size: The font size used to calculate the background rectangle.
    origin: The origin of all coordinates in the output.
    default_foreground: If the span has no foreground color, this is
        substituted.
    default_background: If the span has no background color, this is
        substituted.
    inline_style: If set, the styles (color, font-weight...) for the span
        are included as a `style='...'` attribute. Use `get_svg_styles` to
        get these independently.
    cls: The classname inserted into the `class` attribute of the text tag.

methods.name: slate.span.Span.mutate
documentation: Creates a new Span object, mutated with the given options.

methods.name: slate.span.Span.split
documentation: Splits this span by the given character.

methods.name: slate.span.Span.as_color
documentation: Returns a mutated Span object with the given color.

methods.name: slate.span.Span.as_bold
documentation: Returns a mutated Span object, with `bold` set to the given value.

methods.name: slate.span.Span.as_dim
documentation: Returns a mutated Span object, with `dim` set to the given value.

methods.name: slate.span.Span.as_italic
documentation: Returns a mutated Span object, with `italic` set to the given value.

methods.name: slate.span.Span.as_underline
documentation: Returns a mutated Span object, with `underline` set to the given value.

methods.name: slate.span.Span.as_blink
documentation: Returns a mutated Span object, with `blink` set to the given value.

methods.name: slate.span.Span.as_fast_blink
documentation: Returns a mutated Span object, with `fast_blink` set to the given value.

methods.name: slate.span.Span.as_invert
documentation: Returns a mutated Span object, with `invert` set to the given value.

methods.name: slate.span.Span.as_conceal
documentation: Returns a mutated Span object, with `conceal` set to the given value.

methods.name: slate.span.Span.as_strike
documentation: Returns a mutated Span object, with `strike` set to the given value.

methods.name: slate.span.Span.as_hyperlink
documentation: Returns a mutated Span object, with `hyperlink` set to the given value.

A class to represent a piece of styled text.

Note that spans are immutable, so to get new versions of an existing span you can
use one of the `as_{style_name}` methods, or the more raw `mutate`.
